title: read_verilog is very slow when module has many for iterations in an always block
body:
read_verilog seems to take exponentially longer reading the following module depending on the number of iterations in the for loop:

```
module TestCase(
  input wire clock,

);

localparam COUNT = 1600;

reg buffer[0:COUNT-1];

always @(posedge clock) begin : GENERATE
  integer ii;

  for(ii = 0; ii < COUNT; ii = ii + 1) begin
    buffer[ii] <= 1'b0;
  end
end

endmodule
```

Time spent by 'yosys -p read_verilog test.v':
- 100 iterations -> 0s
- 200 iterations -> 2s
- 400 iterations -> 8s
- 800 iterations -> 36s
- 1600 iterations -> 160s

The OS X performance tools ([Sample of yosys](https://github.com/cliffordwolf/yosys/files/110653/Sample.of.yosys.txt)) seem to implicate Yosys::AST_INTERNAL::ProcessGenerator::removeSignalFromCaseTree(const std::setRTLIL::SigBit &pattern, RTLIL::CaseRule *cs) which is calling Yosys::RTLIL::SigSpec::remove2(const RTLIL::SigSpec &pattern, RTLIL::SigSpec *other).  This form of remove2() calls to_sigbit_pool() to get a poolRTLIL::SigBit.  Inside to_sigbit_pool(), it creates a poolRTLIL::SigBit and then inserts each each bit of each chunk.  This insert seems to trigger a memory allocation via pool<>::insert calling pool<>::do_insert which does a push_back() on a new vector.  It seems that creating a pool on each call to remove2() is excessive in this case.
